_work.title: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
_work.creator: various
_work.place: England, France, Germany, Italy; Islamic world; West Africa; Madagascar
_work.blurb: Geomancy (*geomantia*, 'earth divination'; Arabic *'ilm al-raml*, 'science of the sand') is the art of interpreting patterns generated by random marks in earth or sand, codified into 16 binary figures (Puer, Puella, Acquisitio, Amissio, and twelve others) and read through a system of twelve 'houses' derived from astrology. Transmitted from Arabic sources into Latin Europe through 12th-century translations (Hugo of Santalla, Gerard of Cremona), geomancy became the most systematic of the non-astrological divination arts — precise, teachable, and defensible as natural philosophy. Its companion arts, chiromancy (palmistry) and physiognomy (face-reading), share its diagnostic, text-plus-diagram format. Together these arts produced one of the richest manuscript illustration traditions in medieval and Renaissance Europe: elaborate figure charts, body maps, and casting diagrams survive in hundreds of manuscripts from Paris to Prague. The geomantic tradition also extends far beyond Europe: Arabic *'ilm al-raml* remains a living practice, West African Ifa divination shares the same 16-figure binary logic (as the 256 *odu* of Ifa), and Malagasy *sikidy* preserves the oldest continuous geomantic practice outside the Arab world.

entries.title: Arab Geomantic Figures: The Sixteen
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A classification diagram of the sixteen Arabic geomantic figures (*ashkal al-raml*), the core combinatorial vocabulary of *'ilm al-raml*, 'the science of the sand.'

## Iconography
The diagram arrays the sixteen [[motif:geomancy|geomantic figures]] — each a column of four rows, each row carrying one or two dots — in their traditional groupings. The figures are distinguished by the odd/even parity of each row: one dot = odd (active); two dots = even (passive). The sixteen possible combinations of four binary rows generate every figure in the system, from Puer (single dot in each of the first three rows, two in the fourth) to Via (single dots throughout).

## Significance
*'Ilm al-raml* ('the science of the sand') crystallised in the Arabic-speaking world between the 9th and 12th centuries, drawing on older Mesopotamian and possibly sub-Saharan African precedents. The 12th-century translations of Hugo of Santalla and Gerard of Cremona introduced the figures to Latin Europe as *geomantia*, where they were adopted wholesale, their Arabic names Latinised (Rubeus, Albus, Laetitia, Tristitia, etc.) and their interpretive apparatus fused with European astrology. The sixteen figures have an exact formal parallel in the 256 *odu* of Yoruba Ifa divination (pairs of figures), confirming deep structural links across the Old World.

## For artists & game designers
The canonical reference sheet for all sixteen figures: each a combinatorial icon as compact and legible as a heraldic charge, directly usable as a symbol system in a game or illustration.

citations.text: S. Skinner, *Geomancy in Theory and Practice* (1980).
T. Fahd, 'Geomancy,' in *Encyclopaedia of Islam* (2nd ed.).

entries.title: Sikidy: Working Example Diagram (Ascher)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: After Marcia Ascher
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A schematic working example of *sikidy*, the Malagasy geomantic divination system, drawn after the mathematical ethnologist Marcia Ascher's analysis.

## Iconography
The diagram shows a completed *sikidy* tableau: seeds or beans are arranged in columns, reduced by counting to one-or-two remainders in each of four rows, generating a column of four binary [[motif:geomancy|geomantic figures]]. Multiple columns are combined, producing derived figures through a process of addition (odd + odd = even, odd + even = odd), until the full tableau — mothers, daughters, nieces, and witnesses — is complete.

## Significance
*Sikidy* is the oldest continuously documented geomantic tradition outside the Arab world. Its Malagasy vocabulary and cosmological framework are independent of Arabic *'ilm al-raml*, yet the underlying sixteen-figure binary system is structurally identical. Ascher's mathematical analysis demonstrated that *sikidy* constitutes a sophisticated combinatorial system encoding ecological, calendrical, and cosmological knowledge — not a degenerated import but a fully developed indigenous science. Together with Arabic and African geomantic traditions, *sikidy* shows how widely and independently a binary divination logic can develop.

## For artists & game designers
An oracle generation algorithm as clean as a flowchart: reduce random quantities to odd/even, build figures, combine them. Trivially implementable as a game mechanic with deep historical authenticity.

citations.text: M. Ascher, *Ethnomathematics: A Multicultural View of Mathematical Ideas* (1991), ch. 5.

entries.title: Sikidy Lands: The Sixteen Figures in Malagasy Tradition
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: After Marcia Ascher
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A diagram mapping the sixteen *sikidy* geomantic figures to their associated cosmological 'lands' (*tany*) in Malagasy divination, after Marcia Ascher.

## Iconography
The diagram lays out the full set of sixteen [[motif:geomancy|geomantic figures]] as used in *sikidy*, each named in Malagasy and associated with a region, direction, season, or quality. The names differ from their Arabic and Latin counterparts (the same figure called Acquisitio in Latin geomancy has a distinct Malagasy name and set of associations), showing how the binary figure system was culturally re-coded.

## Significance
The 'lands' system is one of the most distinctive features of *sikidy*: each figure is not merely a sign but a place in a living cosmological map, with associations to time, space, and human fate. Ascher's reconstruction shows the system's internal coherence. The existence of such a fully developed cosmological lattice in Madagascar — an island settled by Austronesians from Southeast Asia and Bantu-speaking peoples from East Africa — raises intriguing questions about the routes by which geomantic knowledge travelled.

## For artists & game designers
A ready-made exotic world-cosmology: sixteen named 'lands' each tied to a direction, season, and quality — a complete geography of fate for a fantasy setting.

entries.title: Sikidy Tableau Diagram (Working Calculation)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: After Marcia Ascher
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A linear tableau diagram showing the complete sequence of a *sikidy* reading: mothers, daughters, nieces, and witnesses generated by binary addition of the sixteen figures.

## Iconography
The strip tableau shows four 'mother' figures (generated from seeds), then four 'daughters' (derived by recombining the mothers' rows), then four 'nieces' (derived from mothers and daughters), then two 'witnesses,' and finally the 'judge.' This is the same cascade structure as European Latin [[motif:geomancy|geomancy]] — mothers (*matres*), daughters (*filiae*), nephews (*nepotes*), witnesses (*testes*), judge (*iudex*) — demonstrating the structural identity between the two traditions.

## Significance
The tableau structure is the geomantic method's most characteristic feature, and its preservation in *sikidy* alongside the identical sixteen-figure binary vocabulary is the strongest evidence for the historical connection between Arab *'ilm al-raml* and Malagasy divination. Whether the method arrived via direct Arab trade contact with the East African coast (from which Madagascar was peopled) or through an older route is debated, but the structural match is undeniable. Ascher's diagram makes the cascade logic transparent.

## For artists & game designers
A clean generative algorithm: four random inputs cascade through a defined transformation into a single output 'judgement' — ideal for a procedurally generated oracle system.

entries.title: Sikidy Divination Session, Madagascar (c. 1895)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous photographer
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A photograph (c. 1895) documenting a *sikidy* divination session in Madagascar, showing a *mpisikidy* (diviner) working with seeds.

## Iconography
The photograph shows one or more figures engaged in a *sikidy* reading — the diviners lay out seeds or beans, counting them down to odd or even remainders to build the [[motif:geomancy|geomantic figures]], then read the resulting tableau. The colonial-era photograph is a rare ethnographic record of the practice in action.

## Significance
Photographic documentation of *sikidy* in the late 19th century catches the tradition at a moment when French colonial rule was consolidating its grip on Madagascar (French conquest was completed in 1895–96). The photograph is part of a broader colonial archive of 'native practices,' though it preserves an invaluable record of a geomantic tradition that remained largely oral and performance-based rather than textual. The *mpisikidy* occupied an important social role in Malagasy society, consulted on questions ranging from health to agriculture to political decisions.

## For artists & game designers
A period image of divination in action: seeds on the ground, a consulting client, a working diviner — a direct source for a living divinatory practice rather than a manuscript diagram.

citations.text: S. Skinner, *Geomancy in Theory and Practice* (1980).

entries.title: Malagasy Diviner with Sikidy at Tamatave (c. 1900)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous photographer
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A photograph (c. 1900) of a Malagasy diviner — captioned 'sorcier malgache' in the colonial French style — practicing *sikidy* at Tamatave on Madagascar's east coast.

## Iconography
The diviner is shown with the materials of *sikidy* practice: seeds arranged for counting, the results being read. The port city of Tamatave (now Toamasina) was a major point of cultural exchange between Madagascar, the East African coast, and the wider Indian Ocean world — appropriately, given the Arabic geomantic roots of *sikidy*.

## Significance
The French colonial label 'sorcier' (sorcerer) betrays the colonial frame: a sophisticated mathematical and cosmological divination system is reduced to sorcery for the colonial gaze. The photograph nonetheless records a *mpisikidy* at work in a specific named location and time, making it a rare piece of evidence for the geographic distribution and social status of [[motif:geomancy|geomantic divination]] in late-19th-century Madagascar. The east coast location is significant: Tamatave's Indian Ocean trade connections are the most likely historical channel through which *'ilm al-raml* reached Madagascar.

## For artists & game designers
A period portrait of a working diviner: the contrast between the colonial label and the sophisticated practice it misnames gives the image a layered historical richness.

entries.title: Berliner Chiromantie: German Palmistry Broadsheet
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous (German)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A German chiromantic broadsheet — the *Berliner Chiromantie* — showing a diagrammatic hand with labelled lines and mounts for palmistry reading.

## Iconography
The open palm is mapped with named [[motif:chiromancy|chiromantic lines]] (Lebens-, Kopf-, and Herzlinie; the Girdle of Venus) and the fleshy mounts at the base of each finger and thumb, each assigned to a planet. Text blocks in German gloss the lines' meanings. The broadsheet format suggests popular distribution: chiromancy was the most widely practised of the 'occult sciences' in the German-speaking world.

## Significance
German chiromantic literature was particularly rich in the 15th and 16th centuries, with major works by Johannes Hartlieb (*Buch aller verbotenen Kunst*, 1456) and Johannes ab Indagine (*Introductiones Apotelesmaticae*, 1522) reaching wide audiences. Broadsheets like this one disseminated chiromantic knowledge to non-Latin readers. The planetary system of mounts — each finger base ruled by Jupiter, Saturn, Apollo, or Mercury, and the thumb mount by Venus — was Agrippa's systematisation of an older, more ad hoc tradition. By the 16th century chiromancy had become a vernacular science, taught in universities and practised by physicians.

## For artists & game designers
A vernacular, popular version of the palm-map: simpler and more direct than the learned Latin diagrams, closer to what a village fortune-teller would have owned.

entries.title: Astrology, Chiromancy & Physiognomy (Wellcome MS, plate 1)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A manuscript page from the Wellcome Collection (L0018732) combining [[motif:chiromancy|chiromancy]], physiognomy, and astrological diagrams in a single folio.

## Iconography
The page brings together the three great body-reading arts of the medieval and Renaissance period: palm diagrams (chiromancy), face or body illustrations (physiognomy), and astrological figures — the standard triumvirate of a learned diviner's handbook. Such compilations were common: the arts were regarded as complementary, each reading a different surface of the same astrologically-inscribed body.

## Significance
The combination of chiromancy, physiognomy, and astrology on a single page reflects the medieval understanding of the body as a microcosm of the heavens. Every line on the palm, every contour of the face, was held to be the imprint of planetary influence at birth. Manuscripts combining all three arts were practical reference tools: the diviner who could read hand, face, and horoscope had a complete forensic picture of the client. The Wellcome Collection holds an unusually rich array of such practical medical-divinatory manuscripts.

## For artists & game designers
A multi-modal divination reference page: the conjunction of palm map, face diagram, and star chart on one folio is a compact archetype for an in-world 'occultist's notebook.'

entries.title: Astrology, Chiromancy & Physiognomy (Wellcome MS, plate 2)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A second manuscript plate from the same Wellcome Collection source (L0018733), continuing the combined presentation of chiromancy, physiognomy, and astrology.

## Iconography
This companion folio develops the diagrammatic apparatus further — additional palm maps, body-part assignments, or facial-feature keys. Such manuscripts typically provided multiple hands or faces labelled with different planetary attributions, allowing the reader to compare types. The scribal and visual tradition of these compilations was remarkably stable across Latin Europe.

## Significance
The two Wellcome plates together illustrate the standard contents of a practical diviner's reference book as compiled in medieval and early modern Europe. The Wellcome Collection preserves many such manuscripts, which were typically owned by physicians, barber-surgeons, and educated laypeople with an interest in prognostic medicine. [[motif:chiromancy|Chiromancy]] and astrology were not clearly separated from medicine: the planetary mounts of the hand, for example, were used diagnostically as well as prognostically, and physiognomy (face-reading) overlapped with humoral medicine.

## For artists & game designers
The second plate from the same notebook: read together, the pair reconstruct a complete working diviner's reference — two openings of a small handbook that was consulted in practice.

entries.title: Hand Covered in Symbols (Wellcome L0068573)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A striking Wellcome Collection image (L0068573) of a hand densely covered in occult symbols — an unusually elaborate example of the divinatory hand-diagram tradition.

## Iconography
The palm and fingers are covered with an unusual density of [[motif:chiromancy|symbolic marks]] — possibly including letters, signs, astrological symbols, or other occult inscriptions beyond the standard planetary line-and-mount system. Such over-marked hands were used in talismanic contexts as well as for palmistry, and the hand itself was a privileged surface for occult inscription across traditions.

## Significance
The hand as a site of occult inscription goes beyond chiromancy proper into the broader traditions of talisman-making, medical astrology, and practical magic. Physicians and diviners used the palm as a mnemonic surface — the 'physician's hand,' the 'chiromantic hand,' and the 'magical hand' overlapped in the medieval medical-occult encyclopedia. The Wellcome example is an unusually rich instance of this over-inscription, closer to a talisman than a simple diagnostic diagram.

## For artists & game designers
The hand as maximalist occult canvas: cover every surface with symbols and it becomes something between a diagram and an amulet — a direct model for game art or character design.

entries.title: Chiromancy and Veterinary Receipts (Wellcome L0015603)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Wellcome Collection manuscript (L0015603) combining chiromantic hand diagrams with veterinary medical recipes — a typically eclectic practical notebook of the medieval or early modern period.

## Iconography
The manuscript page juxtaposes [[motif:chiromancy|chiromancy]] (palm diagram with labelled lines and mounts) with *veterinary receipts* — recipes for treating livestock. The combination is characteristic of the practical medical-occult notebooks kept by rural practitioners, stewards, and householders who needed both to diagnose disease (human and animal) and to interpret natural signs.

## Significance
The conjunction of palmistry and veterinary medicine in a single notebook reveals the practical, synthetic character of popular occult knowledge in medieval and early modern Europe. The reader was not a theorist but a practitioner who needed usable knowledge from multiple domains in one portable reference. Such compilations — chiromancy + herbal + receipts + almanac material — were among the most common manuscript genres. Chiromancy in this context is not mystical entertainment but a diagnostic tool alongside bloodletting charts and livestock remedies.

## For artists & game designers
A reminder that medieval occultism was practical: the diviner who read your palm might also have a cure for your sick ox. A useful texture for a realistic setting.

entries.title: Magnus Hundt, Antropologium (Wellcome L0032471)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Magnus Hundt
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A plate from Magnus Hundt's *Antropologium de hominis dignitate, natura et proprietatibus* (Leipzig, 1501), a proto-anthropological and physiognomical treatise (Wellcome Collection, L0032471).

## Iconography
The woodcut plate illustrates human bodily structure and proportion in the style characteristic of early printed physiognomic literature — schematic figures with labelled anatomical features and possibly a body-proportion diagram. Hundt's *Antropologium* sits at the intersection of scholastic philosophy, natural history, and [[motif:physiognomy|physiognomy]].

## Significance
Magnus Hundt (1449–1519) was a Leipzig physician and philosopher whose *Antropologium* (1501) — an early use of the term — attempted a systematic account of human nature drawing on Aristotle, Galen, and Albert the Great. The work includes physiognomical material and body-diagram conventions later developed by della Porta and others. As one of the earliest printed anthropological treatises, it marks the moment when the medieval compendium tradition (body + soul + stars) begins to transform into something approaching Renaissance humanism. The Wellcome Collection's copy preserves the woodcut illustrations that were the book's primary teaching apparatus.

## For artists & game designers
An early Renaissance body-diagram: the human figure systematically labelled as both anatomical specimen and philosophical subject — a template for an in-world occult textbook plate.

citations.text: M. Hundt, *Antropologium de hominis dignitate, natura et proprietatibus* (Leipzig, 1501).

entries.title: Magnus Hundt, Antropologium (Wellcome L0032472)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Magnus Hundt
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A second plate from Magnus Hundt's *Antropologium de hominis dignitate* (Leipzig, 1501), the Wellcome Collection copy (L0032472).

## Iconography
This companion woodcut develops the body-diagram and [[motif:physiognomy|physiognomical]] apparatus of the preceding plate — a further illustration from the same treatise, likely showing different aspects of the human figure: head and face diagrams, limb proportions, or internal organ schemes. Together the two plates reconstruct the visual content of Hundt's contribution to Renaissance body philosophy.

## Significance
The *Antropologium*'s two Wellcome plates together show the range of the early printed body-diagram tradition, from anatomical-proportional schemes to physiognomic reading. Hundt's work predates the anatomical revolution of Vesalius (*De Humani Corporis Fabrica*, 1543) by forty years; its diagrams are still Galenic and philosophical rather than dissection-based, but they show the same impulse to systematise and illustrate the human body as a legible text. The physiognomical content — reading character from bodily features — connects Hundt directly to the divinatory tradition.

## For artists & game designers
The companion plate: viewed together with L0032471, the two reconstruct a complete illustrated spread from one of the earliest printed 'human science' textbooks.

citations.text: M. Hundt, *Antropologium de hominis dignitate, natura et proprietatibus* (Leipzig, 1501).

entries.title: Mapping Fortunes: A Living Geomantic Reading (India)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Photograph by Meena Kadri
entries.rights: CC BY 2.0. Meena Kadri / Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A contemporary photograph (CC BY 2.0, Meena Kadri) titled 'Mapping Fortunes,' showing a ground-level geomantic or astrological reading in progress in India — dots or marks laid out on a surface as part of a divination session.

## Iconography
The image shows [[motif:geomancy|marks]] or figures drawn on a flat surface — sand, paper, or cloth — in a pattern suggestive of geomantic casting or astrological charting. The 'mapping fortunes' title connects the image to the broader tradition of divination as a spatial practice: fortune is laid out, mapped, read from a two-dimensional field of signs.

## Significance
Geomantic and astrological street-divination remains a living practice across South and Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Africa. The image serves as a reminder that the tradition documented in medieval Latin manuscripts and Islamic treatises has never died: it is still being practiced in contemporary contexts with the same fundamental logic of marks, patterns, and interpreted fate. Connecting the archival record to the living practice is essential for understanding these arts not as historical curiosities but as durable technologies of knowledge.

## For artists & game designers
A contemporary, ground-level divination scene: the same practice as the medieval manuscripts, translated to a modern street context — useful as a reference for realistic portrayal of working divination.

entries.title: Divination Board for Ifa Priest (LACMA)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Yoruba (anonymous carver)
entries.rights: Public domain. LACMA (AC1993.218.3). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An *Opon Ifa* (Ifa divination tray) from the Yoruba tradition, Nigeria, held by the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA, AC1993.218.3).

## Iconography
The circular carved wooden tray is edged with a border of relief-carved figures and decorative motifs — typically including Eshu/Elegba, the divine messenger who must be acknowledged before any Ifa divination can proceed, along with faces, geometric patterns, and animal images. The flat central surface is dusted with powdered wood (*iyerosun*) on which the [[motif:Ifa|divination marks]] (*odu*) are drawn.

## Significance
[[motif:Ifa|Ifa]] divination — the divinatory system of the Yoruba people of southwestern Nigeria — operates through a corpus of 256 *odu* (named sign-complexes, each the union of two of the sixteen primary figures). The structural parallel with Arab-European geomancy's sixteen figures and their combinations is more than coincidental: both systems generate binary figures through a process of random mark-making and odd/even tallying. Whether this represents convergent invention or historical contact (via trans-Saharan or Indian Ocean routes) is debated, but the formal identity is exact. The LACMA board is one of the finest museum examples of the *Opon Ifa*.

## For artists & game designers
A masterwork of divinatory object design: the tray's carved border frames the act of divination as a sacred theatrical event — the marks appear within a cosmologically charged space.

citations.text: LACMA (Los Angeles County Museum of Art), AC1993.218.3.
W. Abimbola, *Ifa: An Exposition of Ifa Literary Corpus* (1976).

entries.title: Divination Board (Pako Ifa), Brooklyn Museum
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Yoruba (anonymous carver)
entries.rights: CC BY 3.0. Brooklyn Museum (22.237). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A *Pako Ifa* (Ifa divination board with handle/stand), Yoruba, Nigeria, from the Brooklyn Museum collection (22.237).

## Iconography
The board is a variant *Opon Ifa* format with an extended handle or foot, making it portable and free-standing. The carved relief decoration on the border and handle displays [[motif:Ifa|Yoruba iconographic]] motifs — human figures, Eshu faces, geometric patterns — framing the divination surface. Such variation in board form reflects local workshop traditions and individual patron requirements.

## Significance
The Brooklyn Museum's Yoruba collection is one of the most significant in North America. The *Pako Ifa* format demonstrates the range of material culture developed around Ifa divination: the basic wooden tray could be an austere functional tool or an elaborately carved prestige object commissioned by a wealthy *babalawo* (Ifa divination priest). The *babalawo* occupies a position of high status in Yoruba society, comparable in some respects to the medieval Latin geomancer who served courts and nobles. The formal parallel between the *babalawo*'s sixteen primary figures and European geomancy's sixteen is a standing scholarly puzzle.

## For artists & game designers
A handled divination board: the variant format suggests portability, travel, the diviner who moves between clients — a useful prop detail for character design.

entries.title: Divination Tray (Opon Ifa), Brooklyn Museum
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Yoruba (anonymous carver)
entries.rights: CC BY 3.0. Brooklyn Museum (75.147.1). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An *Opon Ifa* (Ifa divination tray) from the Brooklyn Museum collection (75.147.1), used by a Yoruba Ifa priest (*babalawo*) to generate and record divinatory signs.

## Iconography
The tray presents a flat circular surface bounded by carved relief decoration. In practice it would be lightly dusted with *iyerosun* (the powdered wood of the *Baphia nitida* tree), and the *babalawo* would use his finger or a divining chain to mark [[motif:Ifa|Ifa figures]] in the dust, recording each *odu* as it appears. The marks are then read against the memorised corpus of *Odu Ifa*, the enormous oral literature associated with each figure.

## Significance
The *Opon Ifa* is the material centre of one of the world's most elaborate divination systems. UNESCO has inscribed Ifa divination on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (2008). The *babalawo* memorises the 256 *odu* and their associated *ese* (poems) — a corpus estimated in the hundreds of thousands of lines — making Ifa one of the largest oral literary traditions ever documented. The formal structure of the sixteen primary figures and their 240 compounds exactly parallels European geomancy's sixteen figures and their shield-chart combinations, one of the most intriguing structural echoes in the history of divination.

## For artists & game designers
The standard form of the Ifa tray: a ritual object that is both tool (the divination surface) and artwork (the carved border). Clean, functional, and numinous.

citations.text: W. Abimbola, *Ifa: An Exposition of Ifa Literary Corpus* (1976).

entries.title: Men Examining a Palm (Wellcome V0025922, engraving by Joseph Haynes)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Joseph Haynes (engraver)
entries.rights: Public domain. Wellcome Collection (V0025922). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An engraving (Wellcome V0025922, attributed to engraver Joseph Haynes) of a group of men seated around a table examining a palm — a scholarly or social palm-reading scene.

## Iconography
Several men in contemporary costume are grouped around a table, one extending his hand while others scrutinise it. The setting suggests a drawing-room or tavern rather than a professional diviner's booth — [[motif:chiromancy|palmistry]] as a social entertainment or serious discussion, not a commercial transaction. The engraving conveys the learned-gentlemanly side of the chiromantic tradition.

## Significance
By the 17th and 18th centuries, palmistry had become a drawing-room accomplishment as well as a professional practice. Works like Richard Saunders' *Palmistry, the Secrets Thereof Disclosed* (1663) circulated in polite society, and the reading of palms was a social performance that could be learned from a book. Joseph Haynes (c. 1654–1701) was an English comedian and engraver associated with theatrical illustration; if this attribution is correct, the scene may have theatrical as well as social resonances. The image captures the genteel, sceptical-yet-curious attitude of Enlightenment-adjacent society towards divinatory arts.

## For artists & game designers
A social scene of palm-reading: not a sinister fortune-teller's booth but a civilised gathering of curious men — the respectable face of the chiromantic tradition.

entries.title: Gypsies Reading the Hand of a Young Traveler (Cornelis de Wael)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Cornelis de Wael
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A genre painting by Cornelis de Wael (1592–1667) showing Romani fortune-tellers reading the palm of a young traveler — one of the most common scenes in 17th-century European genre painting.

## Iconography
A young man extends his hand to a woman (or women) who scrutinise it while others in the group look on. The scene is set outdoors or in a marginal social space — a roadside, a fair, the edge of a city. The [[motif:chiromancy|palm-reading encounter]] between a naive young man and cunning Romani women was a standard genre trope, carrying coded messages about social boundaries, deception, and the lure of the forbidden.

## Significance
Cornelis de Wael worked principally in Antwerp and Italy, and the 'gypsy fortune-teller' genre (pioneered in painting by Caravaggio's *Buona Ventura* of c. 1594) was one of the most popular in 17th-century European art. The genre simultaneously mythologises Romani people as possessors of mysterious divinatory knowledge and figures them as thieves and deceivers. The palm-reading encounter is a social drama: the young man's hand is open, his fortune visible to those who can read it — but will they tell the truth? The paintings raise questions about trust, knowledge, and social hierarchy as much as about divination itself.

## For artists & game designers
The classic encounter scene: innocent hand extended to knowing reader — the palm-reading as moment of vulnerability and revelation.

entries.title: Consulting the Fortune-teller (Gabriel von Max)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Gabriel von Max
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A 19th-century genre painting by Gabriel von Max (1840–1915) showing a woman consulting a fortune-teller — a scene of palm-reading, card-laying, or crystal-gazing.

## Iconography
A young woman is shown in consultation with a fortune-teller, the exchange charged with the mixture of credulity and anxiety that characterises the Romantic genre of [[motif:chiromancy|divinatory encounter]]. Von Max specialised in subjects at the edge of the rational and supernatural — spiritualism, hypnosis, mysticism — and the fortune-teller scene fits naturally into his thematic range.

## Significance
Gabriel von Max (1840–1915), the Czech-German painter, was the pre-eminent painter of the supernatural in the late 19th century, an era fascinated by spiritualism, Theosophy, and the edges of science. His fortune-teller paintings are part of a broader cultural moment when the divinatory arts — discredited by the Enlightenment — were being rehabilitated through the Romantic imagination and later through Spiritualism. The scene of a woman consulting a diviner was gendered in a specific way: women were understood as both more credulous and more receptive to the supernatural, a stereotype that shaped both the practice and the depiction of 19th-century fortune-telling.

## For artists & game designers
A Symbolist/Romantic atmosphere for a divination scene: soft light, anxious client, knowing diviner — the mood of mystery that the 19th century attached to these arts.

entries.title: Gypsy Woman Reading the Hand of a Young Man (Jan Cossiers)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Jan Cossiers
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Flemish Baroque genre painting by Jan Cossiers (1600–1671) of a Romani woman reading the palm of a young man — a companion to the de Wael and Caravaggio tradition of the 'gypsy fortune-teller' genre.

## Iconography
A young man extends his right hand toward a woman who leans close to examine the lines of his palm. Other figures may be present in the background — the group of Romani travellers, or companions of the young man watching the encounter. The [[motif:chiromancy|palm-reading encounter]] is framed as a moment of theatre: the man's fate is in the woman's reading, and the viewer is positioned as an additional witness.

## Significance
Jan Cossiers was an Antwerp painter who spent time in Rome and was influenced by Caravaggio's genre scenes. The 'gypsy fortune-teller' was already an established genre by the 1630s, but Cossiers' versions are notable for their atmospheric handling of light and their relatively sympathetic — or at least non-caricatural — rendering of the Romani figure. Like de Wael's treatment, the painting is as much a social document as a depiction of divination: the encounter dramatises the boundary between settled and nomadic, learned and intuitive, credulous youth and knowing woman.

## For artists & game designers
A high-quality Baroque source for the palm-reading encounter scene: close physical proximity, direct gaze, the exchange of knowledge for coin — the fundamental divinatory transaction.

entries.title: Kitab al-Bulhan: Devils and Supernatural Beings
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous (Abdulhasan al-Isfahani, attrib.)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A miniature from the *Kitab al-Bulhan* ('Book of Wonders,' Bodleian Library, MS Arab. d. 138, late 14th c.) depicting supernatural beings — *jinn*, demons, or devils — associated with astrological and divinatory sections of this Arabic occult encyclopedia.

## Iconography
The [[motif:Islamic occult|miniature]] shows one or more supernatural figures — rendered in the vivid, slightly monstrous style of late medieval Islamic book painting — associated with hours of the day, planets, or divinatory lots. The *Kitab al-Bulhan* is lavishly illustrated with such beings, which are keyed to astrological and prognostic texts.

## Significance
The *Kitab al-Bulhan* (compiled in Baghdad, late 14th century, under the Jalayirid sultans) is one of the most elaborately illustrated Islamic occult encyclopedias to survive. It combines astrology, geomancy (*'ilm al-raml*), lot-books (*zairja*), weather prediction, and demonology in a richly illustrated compendium. The images of supernatural beings index the *decans* (face-rulers) and planetary hours, providing the diviner with a visual catalogue of the spirits governing different times. The manuscript is held by the Bodleian Library, Oxford, and its plates have become widely reproduced in studies of Islamic occultism.

## For artists & game designers
Late medieval Islamic supernatural iconography: vivid demon figures with clearly defined character — each tied to a planet, hour, or omen — a ready-made monster manual from the geomantic tradition.

citations.text: Bodleian Library, Oxford, MS Arab. d. 138 (*Kitab al-Bulhan*), late 14th c.
E. Savage-Smith & M. B. Smith, *Islamic Geomancy and a Thirteenth-Century Divinatory Device* (1980).

entries.title: Kitab al-Bulhan: Birds and Serpents (Omen Illustration)
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Anonymous (Abdulhasan al-Isfahani, attrib.)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A miniature from the *Kitab al-Bulhan* ('Book of Wonders,' Bodleian Library, MS Arab. d. 138, late 14th c.) showing birds swooping on serpents — an [[motif:augury|omen illustration]] from the divinatory sections of this Arabic occult encyclopedia.

## Iconography
Birds are shown attacking or swooping on snakes or serpents below them — a classic omen image. In both Graeco-Roman and Islamic omenology, the sight of birds and serpents in combat was a significant portent, carrying meanings about victory and defeat, the higher principle overcoming the chthonic. The [[motif:Islamic occult|miniature]] is typical of the *Kitab al-Bulhan*'s lavish illustration of its omen and divination texts.

## Significance
The *Kitab al-Bulhan*'s section on ornithomancy (bird omens) and prognostication from natural signs connects the manuscript to the ancient Near Eastern omen tradition (the Babylonian *Enuma Anu Enlil* and related texts) as well as to the Arabic divinatory arts. The image of birds defeating serpents is also cosmologically charged: in Islamic iconography it can figure the victory of spiritual over material, or the triumph of wisdom over earthly dangers. The *Kitab al-Bulhan* weaves these layers of meaning together in a single compendium that a court diviner could consult for omens of every kind.

## For artists & game designers
A medieval Islamic omen image in vivid colour: birds vs. serpents as cosmic drama — directly usable as an omen card or prophetic image in a fantasy setting with Islamic aesthetic influences.

citations.text: Bodleian Library, Oxford, MS Arab. d. 138 (*Kitab al-Bulhan*), late 14th c.

entries.title: Yoruba Divination Board (Fetish, Twins Theme), Nigeria
entries.work: Geomancy, Chiromancy & the Renaissance Divination Arts
entries.creator: Yoruba (anonymous carver; photograph by Mickey Mystique)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0. Mickey Mystique / Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Yoruba divination board (possibly an *Opon Ifa* variant or related divinatory object) from Nigeria, decorated with motifs relating to twins (*Ibeji*) and ancestral figures.

## Iconography
The carved board displays motifs associated with twins (*Ibeji*) and protective figures (*orisa*), alongside standard Yoruba divinatory iconography. Twins hold special significance in Yoruba culture — born at far above the global average rate among the Yoruba, they are considered sacred, and the death of a twin requires the carving of a commemorative figure. The presence of [[motif:Ifa|twin motifs]] on a divination board connects Ifa to the cult of Ibeji and to the broader system of Yoruba religion.

## Significance
The diversity of Yoruba divinatory objects — *Opon Ifa* trays, *opele* (divination chains), *ibo* (casting objects), and associated figures — reflects the complexity and internal differentiation of the Ifa system. Not every Yoruba carving called a 'divination board' functions in exactly the same way: some are specifically *Opon Ifa*, others are associated with particular *orisa* or with protective functions. The twin/Ibeji connection places this board at the intersection of Ifa divination and the cult of sacred twins — one of Yoruba culture's most distinctive features, widely exported across the diaspora to Brazil, Cuba, and the United States.

## For artists & game designers
A divination object dense with symbolic content — the twin motif, the guardian figures, the structured carved surface — a model for a richly characterised magical artefact.

citations.text: R. F. Thompson, *Black Gods and Kings* (1971).
W. Abimbola, *Ifa: An Exposition of Ifa Literary Corpus* (1976).
